file_name: Arora et al. - 2022 - A comparative study of fourteen deep learning networks for multi skin lesion classification (MSLC) o.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep learning and transfer learning with fourteen specific convolutional neural network architectures (AlexNet, GoogleNet, ResNet50, VGG16, VGG19, ResNet101, InceptionV3, InceptionResNetV2, SqueezeNet, DenseNet201, ResNet18, MobileNetV2, ShuffleNet, and NasNetMobile) to classify seven types of skin lesions (including melanoma and basal cell carcinoma) using the ISIC 2018 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the implementation, training, validation, and testing of fourteen specific deep learning networks (AlexNet, GoogleNet, ResNet50, etc.) on the ISIC 2018 dataset. It reports new quantitative results (e.g., accuracy metrics for each model) and proposes specific contributions such as transfer learning with added layers and data augmentation techniques, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: A comparative study of fourteen deep learning networks for multi skin lesion classification (MSLC) on unbalanced data
brief_summary: This study evaluates the performance of fourteen deep learning networks for multi-skin lesion classification using the ISIC 2018 dataset, which contains 10,015 dermoscopic images across seven disease classes. The researchers employed transfer learning on architectures including AlexNet, GoogleNet, ResNet variants, VGGNet, InceptionNet, SqueezeNet, DenseNet201, MobileNetV2, ShuffleNet, and NasNetMobile. Pre-processing steps involved image normalization, resizing, and data augmentation to address class imbalance and improve generalizability. The models were trained and validated with a fixed batch size of 64, 10 epochs, and a learning rate of 0.0001. The results demonstrated that DenseNet201 achieved the highest accuracy of 0.825, outperforming the other thirteen networks. This finding suggests that DenseNet201 is the most effective architecture for automated classification of multiple skin lesions among those tested.

all_task_types.target: Seven diagnostic classes of skin lesions (Actinic keratoses and intraepithelial carcinoma, Basal cell carcinoma, Benign keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Melanoma, Melanocytic type, and Vascular lesions)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multi-class classification of skin lesions using fourteen deep learning networks. The task involves assigning a discrete diagnostic category to dermoscopic images from the ISIC 2018 dataset. Performance is evaluated using accuracy, recall, precision, and F1 score, with DenseNet201 achieving the highest overall accuracy of 0.825.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A public dataset containing dermoscopy images of pigmented lesions from different populations, used for multi-skin lesion classification.

use.training: 70% of the images were used for training.
use.validation: 30% of the images were used for validation and testing (split not specified further).
use.testing: 30% of the images were used for validation and testing (split not specified further).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: No hypothesis testing was reported; the paper compares performance metrics (accuracy, recall, precision, F1 score) across fourteen deep learning networks without statistical significance tests.

architecture_used.main_model: Fourteen deep learning networks: AlexNet, GoogleNet, ResNet50, VGG16, VGG19, ResNet101, InceptionV3, InceptionResNetV2, SqueezeNet, DenseNet201, ResNet18, MobileNetV2, ShuffleNet, and NasNetMobile.
architecture_used.pretraining: Transfer learning was used. Pre-trained networks were modified by adding one fully connected layer, a softmax layer, and a cross-entropy layer for classification into seven classes.
architecture_used.input_size: Images were resized to 224x224x3 or 227x227x3 depending on the network requirement.

architecture_details.DenseNet201: Dense connections between convolutional layers. Consists of four blocks with feature maps of sizes 56x56, 28x28, 14x14, and 7x7. Uses 1x1 bottleneck layers before 3x3 convolutions.
architecture_details.ResNet18: Consists of residual blocks with 3x3 convolutional layers. Starts with 7x7 convolution, 3x3 max pooling, followed by 4 convolutional layers in each module. Total 18 layers including first convolution and last FC layer. Uses batch-wise normalization next to every convolutional layer.
architecture_details.MobileNetV2: Based on separable convolutions (depth-wise and point-wise).
architecture_details.ShuffleNet: Uses depth-wise convolution (3x3, 7x7, 5x5) and dilated convolution (3x3).

training_details.optimizer: Not explicitly named, but implemented in Matlab.
training_details.data_augmentation: Random flipping along vertical axis and shifting up to 30 pixels horizontally and vertically.
training_details.preprocessing: Normalization by subtracting typical RGB values; resizing via bicubic interpolation.

classification_or_output_head.layers: One fully connected layer, one softmax layer, and one cross-entropy layer added to the pre-trained networks.

preprocessing_techniques: Data normalization by subtracting typical RGB values from the dataset
Resizing images using bicubic interpolation to 224x224x3 or 227x227x3 depending on network requirements
Data augmentation via random flipping along the vertical axis
Data augmentation via shifting images up to 30 pixels horizontally and vertically

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Training images were arbitrarily flipped along the vertical axis.
Images were shifted up to 30 pixels horizontally and vertically.

conclusions: DenseNet201 achieved the highest overall accuracy of 0.825 among the fourteen deep learning networks tested, making it the most effective architecture for multi-skin lesion classification in this study.

The study successfully demonstrated that transfer learning with modified fully connected, softmax, and cross-entropy layers can effectively classify seven types of skin lesions using the ISIC 2018 dataset.
Data augmentation techniques, specifically random vertical flipping and shifting images up to 30 pixels, were employed to address class imbalance and improve classification performance.

The comparative analysis of fourteen networks (AlexNet, GoogleNet, ResNet50, VGG16, VGG19, ResNet101, InceptionV3, InceptionResNetV2, SqueezeNet, DenseNet201, ResNet18, MobileNetV2, ShuffleNet, and NasNetMobile) provides a benchmark for automated skin lesion classification models.

The proposed work contributes to building an efficient automated classification model for multiple skin lesions by evaluating various performance parameters including accuracy, recall, precision, and F1 score.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the ISIC 2018 dataset, which exhibits significant class imbalance (e.g., 'Melanocytic type' is the dominant class), potentially biasing model performance and generalizability to other populations.

The dataset split is not fully specified; the paper states 70% for training and 30% for 'validation and testing' combined, but does not clarify if the 30% was further split or if the same data was used for both validation and testing, raising data leakage risks.

No statistical significance testing (e.g., t-tests, confidence intervals) was performed to compare the performance of the fourteen networks, limiting the robustness of the claim that DenseNet201 is superior.

The models were trained for only 10 epochs with a fixed learning rate of 0.0001, which is a very short training duration for deep learning, potentially preventing convergence and underestimating the true potential of the architectures.
The implementation was performed using MATLAB, which may limit reproducibility for researchers accustomed to standard frameworks like PyTorch or TensorFlow.

The study does not report per-class metrics (precision, recall, F1) for the best-performing model (DenseNet201) in the provided text, only overall accuracy (0.825), making it difficult to assess performance on minority classes like Melanoma or Dermatofibroma.

The paper acknowledges that the dataset contains images from 'different populations' but does not provide demographic breakdowns, limiting the assessment of demographic bias in the automated classification model.
